text.fullText: Christopher Phalen Vice President, Domestic Policy February 17, 2026 The Honorable Jessica Kramer Assistant Administrator Office of Water Environmental Protection Agency 1200 Pennsylvania Avenue, N.W. Washington, DC 20460 RE: Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations, 91 Fed. Reg. 2008 (January 15, 2026); Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 Dear Assistant Administrator Kramer: The National Association of Manufacturers (“NAM”) respectfully submits these comments on the proposed rulemaking issued by the Environmental Protection Agency (“EPA” or “the Agency”) entitled, Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations (“the proposed rule” or “the proposal”). The NAM appreciates the opportunity to submit comments on the proposed rule and supports the EPA’s commitment to recalibrating the implementation of Clean Water Act (“CWA”) Section 401 (33 U.S.C. 1341) to realign it with the best statutory reading and congressional intent of the CWA. The NAM is the voice of the manufacturing community and the leading advocate for a policy agenda that supports and empowers the 13 million people who make things in America. As the largest manufacturing association in the U.S., the NAM’s membership includes businesses of all sizes, across all industrial sectors, and in all 50 states. Manufacturers collectively contribute $2.9 trillion to the U.S. economy—and right-sized, commonsense regulations are critical to sustaining the manufacturing strength that underpins our nation’s prosperity. Manufacturers appreciate EPA’s commitment to delivering on the President’s directive to reconsider regulatory developments that present “substantial restraint[s] on our economic growth and ability to build and innovate,” 1 by proposing to “revise several procedural and substantive aspects of the Clean Water Act Section 401 Water Quality Certification Improvement Rule...to address areas of regulatory uncertainty and implementation challenges.”2 The NAM has long called for comprehensive permitting reform, inclusive of regulatory and legislative actions, to drive the growth and competitiveness of manufacturing in the U.S. Modernizing the implementing regulations of the CWA is a key part of such a comprehensive 1 Unleashing Prosperity Through Deregulation, Exec. Order No. 14192, 90 Fed. Reg. 9065 (6 February 2025); Presidential Memorandum: Memorandum for the Heads of Executive Departments and Agencies, Directing the Repeal of Unlawful Regulations (9 April 2025); Repeal of Greenhouse Gas Emissions Standards for Fossil Fuel-Fired Electric Generating Units, 90 Fed. Reg. 25752 (17 June 2025). RIN 2060-AW55; Prevention of Significant Deterioration (PSD) and Nonattainment New Source Review (NNSR): Regulations Related to Project Emissions Accounting; Withdrawal of Proposed Rule, 90 Fed. Reg. 34206 (21 July 2025). RIN 2060-AV62. 2 Proposed rule, at 2008. 1 approach.3 And as the NAM stated in response to the Agency’s 2025 Establishment of Public Docket and Listening Sessions on Implementation Challenges Associated With Clean Water Act Section 401,4 “water quality certification under Section 401 of the CWA has posed challenges in instances when certifying authorities exceed the scope of their statutory mandate” 5 which has “impeded interstate commerce, to the detriment of energy development and manufacturing competitiveness.”6 Manufacturers in America need regulatory certainty and clarity to make capital-intensive investments in federally permitted facilities. Clear timelines and defined review criteria are critical for steel mills and raw material manufacturing and handling facilities that rely on predictable federal permitting schedules. The NAM therefore supports the Agency’s proposal and “a return to appropriate implementation of the requirements of Section 401 of the Clean Water Act, ensuring the certification process remains focused on point source discharges and not a project’s broader scope.”7 Rightsizing the Scope of Certification Reviews The NAM supports the Agency’s proposal to appropriately limit the scope of Section 401 certification reviews to “an assessment of whether a facility's point source discharges into waters of the United States will comply with specified water quality requirements.”8 This change from an activity-based scope is supported by the plain text of the CWA (as amended in 1972) and constitutes the best reading of the statute. See Loper Bright Enterprises v. Raimondo, 603 U.S. 369 (2024). Focusing first on the statutory text of Section 401(a)(1), when read in its entirety, this section does not authorize a state or interstate water pollution control agency to provide a certification of an entire “activity.” 9 Rather, the term “activity” is used in a broader context wherein a state or interstate water quality certification is part of a larger “license or permit.” That certification is limited to “any discharge into the navigable waters” “at the point where the discharge originates or will originate” and ensures that “any such discharge will comply with the applicable provisions of sections 1311, 1312, 1313, 1316, and 1317 of this title.”10 The plain language of this subsection of the CWA demonstrates that the certification authority is not to be applied to an “activity” as a whole, only to point source discharges into the navigable waters (i.e., waters of the United States) that may result from any such activity. 3 America Builds: Clean Water Permitting and Project Delivery Hearing before Subcommittee on Water Resources and Environment, 119th Cong. (February 11, 2025) (statement of Noah Hanners, Executive Vice President, Nucor 4 Establishment of Public Docket and Listening Sessions on Implementation Challenges Associated With Clean Water Act Section 401. 90 Fed. Reg. 29828 (7 July 2025). Available at https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2025/07/07/2025-12564/establishment-of-public-docket-and-listening- sessions-on-implementation-challenges-associated-with 5 See NAM comments on Establishment of Public Docket and Listening Sessions on Implementation Challenges Associated With Clean Water Act Section 401. 90 Fed. Reg. 29828 (7 July 2025). Available at https://www.regulations.gov/comment/EPA-HQ-OW-2025-0272-0046 6 Ibid. 7 Ibid. 8 Proposed rule, at 2026 (emphasis in original). 9 33 U.S.C. 1341(a)(1) 10 33 U.S.C. 1341(a)(1) 2 This statutory interpretation is further supported by Loper Bright. Specifically, the Supreme Court’s decision in Loper Bright obviates previous Chevron deference afforded by the Court to the Agency under its holding in PUD No. 1. of Jefferson County v. Washington Department of Ecology (PUD No. 1).11 The NAM discussed this case previously in our 2019 comments during the first Trump administration’s rulemaking process to right-size the implementation of CWA Section 401.12 As the Agency correctly lays out in the proposal, the Chevron deference afforded in PUD No. 1 was based on ”regulations that predated the 1972 CWA amendments and therefore contained outdated statutory terms, most importantly “activity” rather than “discharge” in CWA Section 401(a)(1).”13 The NAM supports the Agency’s proposal to limit the scope of certification reviews to properly align with statutory authority to “assur[e] that a discharge from a federally licensed or permitted activity will comply with applicable and appropriate water quality requirements.”14 This change will ensure there is no overreach into non-jurisdictional waters and provide much needed clarity for manufacturers by preventing Section 401 from being used to regulate activities unrelated to water quality impacts from the discharge. Certification Decisions Tied to Water Quality Standards Similar to constraining certification reviews to focus exclusively on point source discharges, the NAM also supports the Agency’s proposal to require certification conditions to cite the specific applicable water quality requirement. This will significantly enhance both transparency and enforceability while supporting consistent implementation across regional jurisdictions. As the NAM has noted since 2019, it is clear from the legislative history that Congress specifically intended to address water quality from point source discharges in Section 401 (as previously discussed). 15 Proposing to require state or interstate certifying authorities to expressly disclose if they are granting, granting with conditions, denying, or waiving a certification for an applicable license or permit, with additional supporting information required16 will provide a clear linkage between conditions and regulatory standards. This will help manufacturers understand compliance obligations and avoid conflicting requirements. The NAM therefore respectfully asks the Agency to finalize the proposed changes to CFR 121.7 (c)-(f). 11 511 U.S. 700 (1994). 12 Updating Regulations on Water Quality Certification, Proposed Rule, 84 Fed. Reg. 44,080 (Aug. 22, 2019). Available at https://www.regulations.gov/comment/EPA-HQ-OW-2019-0405-0878 13 Proposed rule, at 2025. 14 Proposed rule, at 2023. 15 See 2019 NAM comment, Available at https://www.regulations.gov/comment/EPA-HQ-OW-2019-0405-0878, pp. 3- 4, Senator Edmund Muskie quotation, etc. 16 In particular, where a certification is denied, ”where certification is denied, the EPA is proposing that the written notification of denial state the reasons for denial, including the specific water quality requirements with which the discharge(s) will not comply; a statement explaining why the discharge will not comply with the identified water quality requirements; or if the denial is due to insufficient information, a description of any missing water quality-related information.” 3 Standardizing Requests for Certification Along with ensuring that the scope of certifications are updated to reflect the correct statutory interpretation, manufacturers support the Agency’s proposal to standardize the contents of a certification request. The proposed changes will improve efficiency, consistency, and predictability in the certification process. Under the current regulatory regime, the process of merely requesting a Section 401 certification is too often used to delay and de facto block critical manufacturing and energy projects—before even reaching discharge and water quality analysis. A plain reading of the statute17 and controlling court precedent18 demonstrate that “receipt” does not require a certification request be “complete.” (Further discussion of manufacturers’ position on the Agency’s proposal regarding timelines is below). The process, beginning upon receipt of the certification request, is the forum through which completeness is to be achieved. Any other reading of the statute that allows sub-federal certifying authorities to impose differing or subjective criteria of “completeness” would obviate the authority granted to the Administrator of the Agency under the CWA. 19 The Agency, in its proposed changes to CFR 121.5 eliminating state- or tribe-specific additional information requirements, provides clear guidelines as to what constitutes a “complete” application upon “receipt.” The NAM supports these proposed revisions, as they will reduce inconsistent practices across jurisdictions affecting permittees, while lowering administrative burden without diminishing water quality protection. To augment the Agency’s proposal in CFR 121.5, the NAM also respectfully requests additional clarification that the standardized list of required materials in 121.5 is intended to be sufficient for certification review and that completeness determinations may not be used to expand the scope of review or delay the start of the statutory review period, including by relying on open-ended language such as “other relevant documentation” in the proposed 121.5(c)(4). Ensuring Appropriate Timelines Returning to the issue of the timeline afforded to certifying authorities to grant, deny, or waive certification, the NAM supports the Agency’s proposal. Firm deadlines are particularly important for major projects subject to Section 401. The statute is clear that, “[i]f the State, interstate agency, or Administrator, as the case may be, fails or refuses to act on a request for certification, within a reasonable period of time (which shall not exceed one year) after receipt of such request, the certification requirements of this subsection shall be waived with respect to such Federal application.”20 The proposal appropriately reinforces the statutory one-year maximum period for action on a certification request. Additionally, removing automatic extensions tied to public notice or force majeure events promotes timely decision-making. The NAM appreciates the Agency proposing 17 33 U.S.C. 1341(a)(1) 18 New York State Department of Environmental Conservation v. Federal Energy Regulatory Commission, 884 F.3d 450 (2d Cir. 2018) 19 “The Administrator is authorized to prescribe such regulations as are necessary to carry out his functions under this chapter.” 33 U.S.C. 1361(a). 20 U.S.C. 1341(a)(1), emphasis added 4 to bar certifying authorities from requesting or compelling applicants to withdraw a request for certification to avoid exceeding the clear one-year statutory requirement. Protecting Applicant Rights in Certification Modifications Manufacturers recognize that some modifications to certifications, or modifications made in the certifying process, are necessary and provide value to projects and water quality outcomes. The current regulations implementing Section 401, however, do not include the project applicant as a formal part of any agreement to proceed with a modification or the final language of such a modification. This has too often led to a lack of trust between certifying authorities and applicants, when applicants ultimately must effectuate any certification modification. It has also proven to be a mechanism by which certifying authorities can misuse the modification process to impose unreasonable modifications on an applicant, in some cases providing de facto certification denials without an authority having to formally deny a project certification as the CWA requires. The NAM therefore supports the Agency’s proposed revisions to CFR 121.10 clarifying the procedures for modifying an issued Section 401 water quality certification. Specifically, providing a defined process for applicant involvement in certification modifications promotes transparency, fairness, and better coordination between applicants and certifying authorities. Additionally, maintaining the prohibition on unilateral modification of certifications by certifying authorities and allowing applicant participation during the modification process will improve predictability for all stakeholders. Providing Predictable Neighboring State Review and Objection Process Finally, regarding the CWA Section 401(a)(2), which prescribes how the Agency notifies “other State[s]” (currently referred to in the CFR as “neighboring jurisdictions”) of potential water quality impacts from a proposed certification in a different state, the NAM supports the proposed rule establishing a 90-day deadline for federal agencies to act on neighboring state objections. This will reduce the open-ended delay risk and ensure the predictable resolution of other states’ objections, which is vital for major projects. The NAM agrees and supports the Agency’s determination that the proposed firm deadlines preserve neighboring state participation while safeguarding timely project decisions. ******** The NAM appreciates the Agency’s consideration of these comments and the Administration’s ongoing engagement with manufacturers throughout the process of preparing this proposal to correctly implement the requirements of CWA Section 401. Manufacturers strongly support a return to appropriately scoped and timed certifications that provide certainty and predictability to manufacturers while reflecting the appropriate balance between federal and state authorities, as Congress intended. Sincerely, Christopher Phalen Vice President, Domestic Policy 5
